en.extract:
The lizard buzzard (Kaupifalco monogrammicus), or lizard hawk, is a bird of prey in the family Accipitridae. It is the only species placed in the genus Kaupifalco. It is native to Sub-Saharan Africa. Despite its name, it may be more closely related to the Accipiter hawks than the Buteo buzzards.


Taxonomy
Molecular phylogenetic studies have revealed that the lizard buzzard is not closely related to Buteo buzzards but rather to Accipiter hawks. This extends to morphological associations, e.g. the lizard buzzard has pointed and fairly short wings. Despite the lizard buzzard inhabiting Africa, phylogenetic evidence indicates that its closest relatives are the two Microspizias hawks of Central and South America.


Description
The lizard buzzard is a smallish stocky raptor with a total body length of 35–37 cm and a wingspan of about 79 cm. Males weigh 246 g, females 304 g on average. The upperparts, head and breast are grey. There is a vertical black line on the white throat, which distinguishes this species from all other raptors. The belly is white with fine dark barring. The underwings are white with dark tips. The tail is black with a white tip and a single white band. The eyes are dark reddish brown to black. The cere and legs are red to orange red. Sexes are similar. The flight pattern is undulating like a thrush. The juvenile lizard buzzard resembles the adult; the only variations are slight brown tinge to the wings with an orange yellow cere and legs.


Distribution and habitat
The lizard buzzard occurs in Sub-Saharan Africa from Eritrea to north eastern South Africa. It remains common in West Africa, Zimbabwe, Mozambique and north eastern areas of Namibia, Botswana and South Africa. Its preferred habitat is moist dense savannah woodland especially miombo woodland, forest edges and wooded margins of rivers. It is also found, especially in winter, in arid thornbush in savannah areas of East and Central Africa.


Ecology
Lizard buzzards are solitary and silent raptors, except for early in breeding season (September and October) when they produce a clear, distinct and melodious whistle klu-klu-klu. They remain locally resident and are dominant over intruders. There is very limited soaring flight, which only occurs during courtship displays or on rare non-breeding occasions in the late morning.
Lizzard buzzards hunt from perches, 6–10 m in height, and catch prey by swooping or gliding onto prey in the grass. They have a low attack rate, passively searching for prey which is energetically inexpensive but time-consuming. They rarely catch prey in flight. They have shorter pointed wings (ratio of wing length to body height 0.76) resulting in a more rapid flight in forests which suggests an adaptation to prey capture in dense vegetation. Their diet consists of invertebrates, reptiles and mammals. By number most common prey are grasshoppers and termites, while by biomass rodents are the most frequent. Reptiles favoured are lizards especially Mabuya and Agama lizards, frogs and snakes.


Breeding
Breeding occurs during the months of September to November. Lizard buzzards are monogamous and form pair bonds that are protected or permanent. Both sexes are involved in nest building which is small and compact, composed of sticks and found in the sub canopy of trees both indigenous and alien, often near the main trunk of the tree. The stick nest is lined with dry grass, green leaves or lichen.
Like some other raptors, lizard buzzards are alternative nest users. Although they prefer to build nests in the subcanopy, when they occupy an existing nest this can occur in the canopy above. Lizard buzzards will also compete with Shikras for available nests, since these have a similar size, habitat preference and distribution.
The clutch size is 1–3 white eggs which are incubated by the female and take 32–34 days before hatching. During this time the male feeds the female and both feed the chicks for the next 40 days. Full independence of the chicks occurs at about 90 days.


Conservation
The range of lizard buzzards is extremely large and does not approach the threshold for vulnerable under range distribution. The population trend appears to be stable and does not approach thresholds for vulnerable. The population size is extremely large and for these reasons is evaluated as least concern. However, in Africa particularly West and Southern Africa there have been recorded dramatic declines in some raptors. The reasons for raptor decline in Africa are rapid human population growth driving overexploitation of the land causing biodiversity loss and a decreased species richness. The raptor population declines in West Africa have been linked to loss of woodland and nest sites, increased pesticide use, intensive cultivation especially cotton and disturbance of nests. In Southern Africa raptor decline has been linked to use of poisons, powerline electrocutions, habitat destruction and raptor drowning in farm reservoirs. Despite high human population and activity in Africa, not all raptor species have declined sharply. Some raptors, especially opportunistic generalists and migrants have increased. In West Africa the grasshopper buzzard, black kite and hooded vulture have increased in number and range. Similarly in the Western Cape of South Africa, the steppe buzzard, lesser kestrel and yellow-billed kite have increased range and number. Currently there is insufficient data on the Lizard Buzzard to determine its adaptation to human land usage. It is unclear how it has adapted to loss of Woodland its preferred habitat and nesting site. Its preferred prey insects, lizards and rodents remain common in most human altered landscapes which could account for its current survival.


References


External links
 Media related to Kaupifalco monogrammicus at Wikimedia Commons
 Data related to Kaupifalco monogrammicus at Wikispecies

[[File:KmonogrammicusGambia.jpg|thumb|K. monogrammicus in The Gambia, December 2021]]

The '''lizard buzzard''' ('''''Kaupifalco monogrammicus'''''), or '''lizard hawk''', is a [[bird of prey]] in the family [[Accipitridae]]. It is the only species placed in the [[genus]] '''''Kaupifalco'''''. It is native to Sub-Saharan Africa. Despite its name, it may be more closely related to the ''[[Accipiter]]'' hawks than the ''[[Buteo]]'' buzzards.

==Taxonomy==
Molecular phylogenetic studies have revealed that the lizard buzzard is not closely related to ''[[Buteo]]'' buzzards but rather to ''[[Accipiter]]'' hawks.<ref>{{Cite journal|last=Lerner|first=Heather|date=29 March 2016|title=Molecular Phylogenetics of the Buteonine Birds of Prey|volume=125|issue=2|pages=304–315|journal=The Auk|doi=10.1525/auk.2008.06161 |s2cid=264252106 |doi-access=free}}</ref> This extends to morphological associations, e.g. the lizard buzzard has pointed and fairly short wings.<ref name=":2">{{Cite book|title=Beat About the Bush Birds|last=Carnaby|first=Trevor|publisher=Jacana Media (Pty) Ltd.|year=2009|location=Johannesburg, South Africa|page=548}}</ref> Despite the lizard buzzard inhabiting Africa, phylogenetic evidence indicates that its closest relatives are the two ''[[Microspizias]]'' hawks of [[Central America|Central]] and [[South America]].''<ref name=":12">{{Cite journal |last1=Sangster |first1=George |last2=Kirwan |first2=Guy M. |last3=Fuchs |first3=Jérôme |last4=Dickinson |first4=Edward C. |last5=Elliott |first5=Andy |last6=Gregory |first6=Steven M. S. |date=2021-02-08 |title=A new genus for the tiny hawk Accipiter superciliosus and semicollared hawk A. collaris (Aves: Accipitridae), with comments on the generic name for the crested goshawk A. trivirgatus and Sulawesi goshawk A. griseiceps |url=https://vertebrate-zoology.arphahub.com/article/67501/ |journal=Vertebrate Zoology |language=en |volume=71 |pages=419–424 |doi=10.3897/vz.71.e67501 |issn=2625-8498 |doi-access=free }}</ref>''

==Description==
The lizard buzzard is a smallish stocky raptor with a total body length of 35–37 cm and a wingspan of about 79 cm. Males weigh 246 g, females 304 g on average. The upperparts, head and breast are grey. There is a vertical black line on the white throat, which distinguishes this species from all other raptors. The belly is white with fine dark barring. The underwings are white with dark tips. The tail is black with a white tip and a single white band. The eyes are dark reddish brown to black. The cere and legs are red to orange red. Sexes are similar. The flight pattern is undulating like a thrush. The juvenile lizard buzzard resembles the adult; the only variations are slight brown tinge to the wings with an orange yellow cere and legs.<ref name=":3"/>
<gallery>
File:Boda madfallaod.jpg|''K. monogrammicus'' in  Uganda
File:Kaupifalco monogrammicus.jpg|''K. monogrammicus'' in [[Saloum Delta National Park]] in [[Senegal]]
File:Lizard buzzard (38130337692).jpg
</gallery>

==Distribution and habitat==
The lizard buzzard occurs in Sub-Saharan Africa from Eritrea to north eastern South Africa. It remains common in West Africa, Zimbabwe, Mozambique and north eastern areas of Namibia, Botswana and South Africa.<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/accipitridae/kaupifalco-monogrammicud.htm|title=Kaupifalco monogrammicus|last=Hockey|first=Dean|date=4 March 2010|website=Biodiversity Explorer|publisher=Iziko museum of Cape Town|access-date=2 April 2016}}</ref><ref name=":1">{{Cite journal|last=Thiollay|first=J.M.|date=15 June 1988|title=Comparative foraging adaptations of small raptors in a dense African savanna|volume=132|journal=Ibis|pages=42–47|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919x.1990.tb01015.x}}</ref> Its preferred habitat is moist dense savannah woodland especially miombo woodland, forest edges and wooded margins of rivers.<ref name=":1" /> It is also found, especially in winter, in arid thornbush in savannah areas of East and Central Africa.<ref name=":3">{{Cite book|title=The Raptor Guide of Southern Africa|last=Oberprieler|first=Ulrich|publisher=Craft International|year=2012|isbn=978-0-9921701-0-3|location=Singapore|page=170}}</ref><ref name=":0" />

==Ecology==
Lizard buzzards are solitary and silent raptors, except for early in breeding season (September and October) when they produce a clear, distinct and melodious whistle ''klu-klu-klu''.<ref name=":3" /> They remain locally resident and are dominant over intruders. There is very limited soaring flight, which only occurs during courtship displays or on rare non-breeding occasions in the late morning.<ref name=":1" />

Lizzard buzzards hunt from perches, 6–10 m in height, and catch prey by swooping or gliding onto prey in the grass. They have a low attack rate, passively searching for prey which is energetically inexpensive but time-consuming.<ref name=":1"/> They rarely catch prey in flight. They have shorter pointed wings (ratio of wing length to body height 0.76)<ref name=":1"/> resulting in a more rapid flight in forests which suggests an adaptation to prey capture in dense vegetation.<ref name=":2" /> Their diet consists of invertebrates, reptiles and mammals. By number most common prey are grasshoppers and termites, while by biomass rodents are the most frequent. Reptiles favoured are lizards especially ''Mabuya'' and ''Agama'' lizards, frogs and snakes.<ref name=":1" />

===Breeding===
Breeding occurs during the months of September to November. Lizard buzzards are monogamous and form pair bonds that are protected or permanent.<ref name=":2" /> Both sexes are involved in nest building which is small and compact, composed of sticks and found in the sub canopy of trees both indigenous and alien, often near the main trunk of the tree. The stick nest is lined with dry grass, green leaves or lichen.<ref name=":2" /><ref name=":3" />

Like some other raptors, lizard buzzards are alternative nest users.<ref name=":4">{{Cite book|title=Raptor survey and monitoring|last=Malan|first=Gerard|publisher=BRIZA PUBLICATIONS|year=2009|isbn=978-1-920146-03-0|location=Pretora, South africa|page=58}}</ref> Although they prefer to build nests in the subcanopy, when they occupy an existing nest this can occur in the canopy above. Lizard buzzards will also compete with Shikras for available nests,<ref name=":4" /> since these have a similar size, habitat preference and distribution.<ref name=":3" />

The clutch size is 1–3 white eggs which are incubated by the female and take 32–34 days before hatching.<ref name=":3" /> During this time the male feeds the female and both feed the chicks for the next 40 days. Full independence of the chicks occurs at about 90 days.<ref name=":3" />

==Conservation==

The range of lizard buzzards is extremely large and does not approach the threshold for vulnerable under range distribution. The population trend appears to be stable and does not approach thresholds for vulnerable. The population size is extremely large and for these reasons is evaluated as least concern. However, in Africa particularly West and Southern Africa there have been recorded dramatic declines in some raptors.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last=Thiollay|first=Jean-Marc|date=3 July 2007|title=Raptor declines in West Africa:comparison between protected, buffer and cultivated areas|journal=Oryx|volume=41 |issue=3 |pages=322–329 |doi=10.1017/S0030605307000809 |s2cid=86157693 |doi-access=free}}</ref><ref name=":6">{{Cite journal|last=Herremans|first=M|date=12 November 2009|title=Roadside abundance of raptors in the Western Cape Province, South Africa:a three-decade comparison|journal=Ostrich|doi=10.2989/00306520109485291|volume=72|issue=1–2|pages=96–100|s2cid=84144800}}</ref> The reasons for raptor decline in Africa are rapid human population growth driving [[overexploitation]] of the land causing [[biodiversity loss]] and a decreased species richness.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6" /><ref name=":7">{{Cite journal|last=Buij|first=Ralph|date=2 August 2012|title=The role of breeding range, diet, mobility and body size in associations of raptor communities and land-use in a West African savanna|journal=Biological Conservation|doi=10.1016/j.biocon.2013.06.028|volume=166|pages=231–246|hdl=11370/f5a2a5fd-5550-4ce9-9bd3-af3987577058|hdl-access=free}}</ref> The raptor population declines in West Africa have been linked to loss of woodland and nest sites, increased pesticide use, intensive cultivation especially cotton and disturbance of nests.<ref name=":5" /><ref name=":7" /> In Southern Africa raptor decline has been linked to use of poisons, powerline electrocutions, [[habitat destruction]] and raptor drowning in farm reservoirs.<ref name=":4" /><ref name=":6" /><ref>{{Cite journal|last=Anderson|first=Mark|date=11 October 2010|title=Raptor drowning in farm reservoirs in South Africa|journal=Ostrich|doi=10.1080/00306525.1999.9634530|volume=70|issue=2 |pages=139–144}}</ref> Despite high human population and activity in Africa, not all raptor species have declined sharply. Some raptors, especially opportunistic generalists and migrants have increased. In West Africa the [[grasshopper buzzard]], [[black kite]] and [[hooded vulture]] have increased in number and range.<ref name=":7" /> Similarly in the Western Cape of South Africa, the [[steppe buzzard]], [[lesser kestrel]] and [[yellow-billed kite]] have increased range and number.<ref name=":6" /> Currently there is insufficient data on the Lizard Buzzard to determine its adaptation to human land usage. It is unclear how it has adapted to loss of Woodland its preferred habitat and nesting site. Its preferred prey insects, lizards and rodents remain common in most human altered landscapes which could account for its current survival.
